topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on the screen, a jarring intrusion in the quiet hum of my evening. I stared at it, my thumb hovering over the ‘Accept’ button, a heavy, sluggish feeling settling in my chest. It was a profile picture I hadn't seen in over a decade—a smirk that used to feel like a physical blow. Mark. The name alone was enough to make my shoulders tense up, a familiar knot tightening beneath my worn sweater. I traced the outline of the request with my gaze, feeling a dull ache behind my eyes. Why now? I didn't really care enough to investigate, just to see what kind of unwelcome ripple this would cause in the stagnant pool of my current mood. I sighed, a sound that seemed too loud in the silence of my room, and finally clicked.

[story 2]

I hadn't moved from the sofa in an hour, the remote forgotten on the cushion beside me. The phone vibrated, pulling my attention away from the muted television. A friend request. My stomach clenched, a tight, unpleasant squeeze. It was him. Liam. The memory of his sneering face, the way he used to corner me by the lockers, surfaced unbidden, bringing a wave of heavy inertia over me. My fingers trembled slightly as I navigated to the message thread. I reread the request, the casual nature of it grating on my nerves. I didn't want interaction; I wanted the quiet to deepen, the comfortable numbness to envelop me entirely. After a long pause, I deleted the request without a second thought, the screen instantly returning to its blank, predictable state.

[story 3]

The blue light of the notification illuminated the dust motes dancing in the air above my desk. I didn't move my head, just watched the message accumulate. It was from him. The sheer audacity of it made my jaw slacken slightly. A familiar, unwelcome pressure built behind my sternum. I felt profoundly unmotivated, as if moving required an impossible expenditure of energy. I could feel the weight of the past pressing down, suffocating the small space I occupied. My breath hitched, shallow and uneven. I let the phone sit there, cold against my palm, unwilling to engage with whatever ghost from my youth was trying to knock on my door. The silence in the room amplified the dull throb in my temples.
